summary: Gruber calls out Tynt, a company whose JavaScript service hijacks the clipboard by appending tracking URLs and attribution text to anything users copy from websites. He documents how major sites like TechCrunch and The New Yorker have adopted this behavior, which breaks the fundamental expectation of how copy and paste works. He argues the tracking value is dubious since most people delete the appended text, and that publishers are burning reader goodwill for negligible analytics. He provides practical instructions for blocking Tynt system-wide by editing the /etc/hosts file.
key_points: Tynt's service silently modifies copied text by appending attribution URLs and tracking codes, fundamentally breaking how copy and paste is expected to work
Major publications including The New Yorker and TechCrunch adopted the service despite it being hostile to their own readers
The tracking value is dubious because most users delete the appended attribution text as soon as they notice it
TechCrunch's adoption is especially ironic since they frequently quote text from other websites and would find the same behavior annoying
Users can block Tynt system-wide by adding a hosts file entry redirecting tcr.tynt.com to 127.0.0.1
money_quotes: It's a bunch of user-hostile SEO bullshit.

Everyone knows how copy and paste works. You select text. You copy. When you paste, what you get is exactly what you selected. The core product of the "copy/paste company" is a service that breaks copy and paste.
It certainly isn't being appended to help the person copying and pasting the text. The person copying the text knows where it comes from.
Their websites are theirs, but our clipboards are ours.
Tynt is intrusive, obnoxious, and disrespectful. I can't believe some websites need to be told this.
Whatever their justification for using Tynt is, I'll bet it involves repeated use of the phrase "biz dev". All they're really doing is annoying their readers.
They're burning some measure of goodwill from their readers in exchange for URL tracking analytics from Tynt identifiers that most people, I bet, delete as soon as they see them after pasting.
key_insight: Tynt exemplifies how publishers sacrifice user trust and break fundamental computing conventions for dubious analytics value, treating readers' clipboards as their own property.
